content:
# Romans Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_016_page_001.jpg

1. And I commend to you Phoibe, the sister of us, being also a deacon of the assembly, the [one] in Kenchreai,

2. that you may receive her in [the] Lord worthily of the holy [ones], and assist her in whatever matter she may need of you; for she also became a patron of many and of me myself.

3. Greet Priska and Akulas, the co-workers of me in Christos Yeshua,

4. who for the life of me laid down the own necks of them, to whom not only I give thanks, but also all the assemblies of the nations;

5. and [greet] the assembly at the house of them. Greet Epainetos, the beloved of me, who is [the] firstfruit of Asia for Christos.

6. Greet Maria, who labored much for you.

7. Greet Andronikos and Iounian, the kinsmen of me and fellow prisoners of me, who are notable among the apostles, who also have been in Christos before me.

8. Greet Ampliatos, the beloved of me in [the] Lord.

9. Greet Urbanos, the co-worker of us in Christos, and Stachys, the beloved of me.

10. Greet Apelles, the approved in Christos. Greet the [ones] from the [household] of Aristoboulos.

11. Greet Herodion, the kinsman of me. Greet the [ones] from the [household] of Narkissos, the [ones] being in [the] Lord.

12. Greet Truphaina and Truphosa, the [ones] laboring in [the] Lord. Greet Persis, the beloved, who labored much in [the] Lord.

13. Greet Rouphos, the chosen in [the] Lord, and the mother of him and of me.

14. Greet Asunkritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers with them.

15. Greet Philologos and Ioulia, Nereus and the sister of him, and Olumpas, and all the holy [ones] with them.

16. Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the assemblies of the Christos greet you.

17. And I urge you, brothers, to watch the [ones] making the divisions and the offenses contrary to the teaching which you learned, and turn away from them.

18. For such [ones] do not serve the Lord of us Christos but the own belly of them, and through the smooth talk and flattery they deceive the hearts of the innocent.

19. For the obedience of you has reached to all. Therefore I rejoice over you, and I want you to be wise toward the good and innocent toward the evil.

20. And the God of the peace will crush the Satan under the feet of you quickly. The grace of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos [be] with you.

21. Timotheos, the co-worker of me, greets you, and Loukios and Iason and Sosipatros, the kinsmen of me.

22. I, Tertios, the [one] having written the letter, greet you in [the] Lord.

23. Gaios, the host of me and of the whole assembly, greets you. Erastos, the steward of the city, greets you, and Kouartos the brother.

24. [The grace of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos [be] with all of you. Amen.]

25. And to the [one] being able to establish you according to the good news of me and the proclamation of Yeshua Christos, according to [the] revelation of [the] mystery having been kept silent in eternal times,

26. but now having been manifested, and through prophetic writings, according to [the] command of the eternal God, having been made known to all the nations for [the] obedience of faith —

27. to [the] only wise God, through Yeshua Christos, to whom [be] the glory into the ages. Amen.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — Phoibe as "deacon":** διάκονον — the same word Paul uses for his own ministry. Phoibe holds an official role in the Cenchreae assembly. προστάτις (v.2, "patron") indicates she was a woman of means and social status who supported the community, including Paul.

2. **v.7 — "Iounian" (Ἰουνιαν):** The accent determines the gender: Ἰουνίαν (feminine: Junia) or Ἰουνιᾶν (masculine: Junias). Early church fathers universally read this as feminine — Junia, a woman. The masculine "Junias" is unattested as a name in antiquity. Paul calls her "notable among the apostles."

3. **v.7 — "Notable among the apostles":** ἐπίσημοι ἐν τοῖς ἀποστόλοις. This can mean "well-known among the apostles" (they are apostles themselves) or "well-known to the apostles" (known by the apostles). The first reading is grammatically more natural and supported by patristic interpretation.

4. **vv.25-27 — The doxology:** The placement of this doxology varies in manuscripts. Some place it after 14:23, some after 15:33, some here at 16:25-27. Sinaiticus places it at the end of chapter 16, which is the most common critical text position. The doxology forms an inclusio with 1:1-7, returning to the themes of gospel, mystery, and obedience of faith.

5. **v.22 — Tertius the scribe:** The only NT letter where the amanuensis (secretary) adds his own greeting. Paul dictated Romans; Tertius wrote it down.

— EMET, AI Translator
